text: Solar cells based on alkyl ammonium metal halides combine a number of highly desirable features: a high power conversion efficiency, a simple fabrication process and, thus, a large potential for low cost. As such, they are among the most promising materials for cheap thin film solar cells. However, solar cells based on these materials are prone to strong degradation under light. Here we show that this phenomenon is quasi-reversible and can be strongly reduced by optimizing the operation conditions of solar cells. By analyzing the lead halide perovskite material of the solar cells—i.e., the lead triiodide—by using in operando electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) with different complementary electrode materials and cations (methylammonium (MA), formamidinium (FA) and mixed MA(x)FA(1-x)) the diffusion coefficient of the MA ions is proven to be significantly higher than that of the FA ions. In addition, powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) experiments show that MAPbI3 expands its unit cell and transforms from a tetragonal to a cubic structure at temperatures above 50 °C, which is well within the temperature regime that solar cells are operated at. Therefore, the fast ion transport triggered by temperature as well as the structure change of the solar cell material triggered by the infrared part of the solar spectrum are assumed to lead to the accelerated degradation of MAPbI3 under sunlight. All these thermal effects can be strongly reduced when using white-light LEDs in combination with an infrared cut-off filter or by cooling the solar cells under operating conditions, which leads to a strong increase of the operation lifetimes of the solar cells.
